Indonesia reversed plans to retire the 660 MW Cirebon-1 coal plant, which had been slated to close by early 2035 under an internationally backed Just Energy Transition Partnership (JETP), prompting frustration among local fishing communities and activists. Residents report disappearing mussels and reduced fish catches they attribute to wastewater and thermal discharge from the plant, and describe increased respiratory problems; some workers, however, rely on the plant for their livelihoods.

The U-turn highlights wider problems with the JETP and Indonesia’s energy transition: pledged funding has grown but only a small portion is available, Washington pulled out and Germany has stepped up to co-lead with Japan, and the government defended keeping Cirebon-1 because of its “supercritical” technology and concerns about electricity prices and uncertain replacement capacity funding. Coal still supplies nearly 70% of Indonesia’s electricity, studies link emissions around Cirebon-1 to more than 400 deaths a year, and analysts warn the move sends a mixed signal on commitment to phasing out fossil-fuel power.

Key facts and figures: Cirebon-1 is 660 MW, began operation in 2012 under a 30-year contract; a second 1,000 MW unit started in 2023; planned early retirement was reversed in December; the JETP commitment has risen to about US$21.8 billion but only roughly US$3.4 billion is available; the US withdrew from the JETP last year while Germany and Japan now co-lead; CREA linked plant emissions to over 400 annual deaths; PLN projects about 16.6 GW of new coal and gas capacity by 2034 and captive industrial plants could add 31 GW; government rationale cites the plant’s efficient “supercritical” technology and funding/price concerns, while plant owners say wastewater meets regulations.
importance_reasoning: The article cites a study linking emissions from the Cirebon-1 coal plant to over 400 annual deaths, which exceeds the API’s >100-deaths threshold for being considered existentially important. While this is a regional, ongoing environmental-health and policy issue rather than an acute global catastrophe (e.g., nuclear war or a novel pandemic), the sustained mortality and its implications for Indonesia’s energy transition and international climate finance meet the endpoint’s criteria for existential importance.

title: Machine guns to machetes: Weapons that massacred thousands in Iran
summary:
BBC Persian analysed hundreds of verified videos and photographs from January protests across more than 200 Iranian cities and found security forces used a wide range of lethal and less‑lethal weapons against demonstrators. The crackdown, described as unprecedented in modern Iranian history, is linked in reports and witness accounts to thousands of deaths and widespread severe injuries.

Verified footage and expert analysis identify specific weapon systems and documented consequences: machine guns (heavy DShK and medium PK) mounted on IRGC‑style pickup trucks used in sustained fire; 20mm automatic‑cannon rounds and AK‑47/7.62x39 casings recovered; Dragunov (SVD) sniper rifles observed on rooftops with forensic evidence of precision headshots; assault rifles and shotguns (including Iranian Maher models) extensively deployed, producing mass eye injuries with CT scans showing metal pellets and a single hospital reporting about 1,000 emergency eye surgeries over 9–10 January; plainclothes agents wielding pistols and machetes; and beatings with batons and gun butts that caused deaths.

Outline of most salient, new and informative facts: verified presence of heavy and medium machine guns (DShK/PK) mounted on pickup trucks with visible muzzle fire; recovery and expert identification of a 20mm automatic‑cannon round (ordnance intended for vehicles) fired at protesters; rooftop deployment of Dragunov snipers with photographic and forensic confirmation of precision shots; confirmed use of AK‑47/KL assault rifles (7.62x39 casings found) and direct firing at crowds; widespread shotgun use (including Maher models) linked to large numbers of ruptured eyeballs and CT evidence of embedded pellets; videos showing plainclothes agents using machetes and pistols; verified fatal beatings with batons and gun butts; and use of non‑ballistic tools such as pellet‑firing paintball devices, tear gas and green lasers for blinding/targeting.
importance_reasoning: Verified reporting links the events to thousands of deaths across more than 200 cities and documents the use of military-grade weapons and organized lethal force against civilians. The scale and lethality (well above the >100 deaths threshold) and the systemic, violent crackdown meet the criteria for existential importance.

title: UN says at least 6,000 killed in 3 days during attack on Sudan's el-Fasher
summary:
A U.N. Human Rights Office report documents that more than 6,000 people were killed during a multi-day offensive by the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) and allied Arab militias in el‑Fasher, North Darfur, in late October. The RSF overran the city after an 18‑month siege and carried out mass killings, summary executions, widespread sexual violence, abductions for ransom, torture, detention, disappearances and ethnicity‑motivated attacks that the report says amount to war crimes and possible crimes against humanity.

The report is based on interviews with 140 victims and witnesses and is corroborated by analysis of contemporaneous satellite imagery and video. It documents at least 4,400 people killed inside el‑Fasher between Oct. 25–27 and more than 1,600 killed while trying to flee, and warns the true toll is likely higher. Notable incidents include heavy weapons fire on about 1,000 people sheltering in the Rashid dormitory (around 500 killed), the execution of roughly 600 people (including about 50 children) in university facilities, around 300 killed by shelling and drone attacks in the Abu Shouk displaced‑persons camp, and at least 460 killed when RSF fighters stormed the Saudi Maternity hospital on Oct. 28. Sexual violence, including rape and gang rape, was reportedly used systematically against women and girls from the African Zaghawa non‑Arab tribe. Thousands were held in at least 10 RSF‑run detention centers, including a children’s hospital; several thousand remain missing.

Outline of salient facts: 6,000+ killed in late‑October el‑Fasher offensive; 4,400 killed inside between Oct. 25–27 and 1,600+ killed while fleeing; ~500 killed at Rashid dormitory; ~600 (including ~50 children) executed in university facilities; ~300 killed in Abu Shouk camp (Oct. 23–24); 460 killed at Saudi Maternity hospital on Oct. 28 (not included in the 6,000 toll); widespread, systematic sexual violence targeting Zaghawa women and girls; thousands detained across ≥10 RSF detention sites; evidence from 140 interviews plus satellite/video; RSF/Janjaweed named as perpetrators, Gen. Mohammed Hamdan Dagalo has acknowledged abuses but disputed scale; U.N. calls for accountability, ICC is investigating, and the conflict has created a large humanitarian crisis with parts of Sudan pushed into famine.
importance_reasoning: This describes a mass‑killing event with well over 100 deaths (UN reports 6,000+ killed), widespread atrocities (summary executions, systematic sexual violence), large-scale displacement and potential crimes against humanity/war crimes. Such large casualties and grave abuses meet the threshold for existential importance because they threaten large numbers of people and signify a major escalation with severe humanitarian and geopolitical consequences.

title: U.S. sanctions Iranian security officials amid deadly protests
summary:
The United States has imposed sanctions on five Iranian security officials, including Ali Larijani, blacklisted the Fardis women’s prison, and targeted an alleged shadow banking network as a show of support for Iranians protesting the government amid a violent crackdown. The Treasury and State Departments, citing direction from President Trump and statements by Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent and State Department spokesperson Thomas Pigott, said the measures are intended to deny the regime access to financial networks and the global banking system.

The actions block any U.S. property of those named and prohibit U.S. persons from doing business with them; the Treasury accused Larijani of calling for and coordinating violent suppression of the protests on behalf of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei. Rights groups report thousands killed and around 20,000 arrested during protests triggered by Iran’s economic crisis, and supporters of the sanctions—including former U.S. ambassador Mark Wallace—praised the designation of Larijani and others.

Outline of key facts: Sanctions target Ali Larijani (67), four other security officials, the Fardis Prison, and 18 individuals/entities tied to a ‘‘shadow banking’’ network allegedly facilitating tens of billions in annual trade; Oslo-based Iran Human Rights reports at least 3,428 protesters killed while Human Rights Activists News Agency reports 2,677 killed with 1,693 additional cases under investigation and roughly 20,000 arrests; U.S. measures freeze U.S. assets and bar U.S. persons from transactions with the listed parties; U.S. officials framed the sanctions as support for Iranian protesters and a means to cut off regime access to international finance; some U.S. critics of the Iranian leadership publicly endorsed the sanctions.
importance_reasoning: The report documents thousands of protester deaths and mass arrests in Iran (well above the 100-death threshold), plus a violent government crackdown and international sanctions targeting senior security officials and institutions. The high casualty count alone meets the API's criterion for existential importance; the events also carry risk of wider political instability and international escalation. Therefore this item is classified as existentially important.

title: Islamic school students' learning fully resumed in Sumatra: Minister
summary:
Religious Affairs Minister Nasaruddin Umar announced that learning for madrasah students in flood- and landslide-hit provinces of Sumatra has fully resumed, with all students continuing education as of Feb. 11, 2026. While about 90.5% of students have returned to normal classes, the remainder are learning via shift systems, emergency tents, or shared facilities as recovery of schools and religious sites progresses.

Restoration efforts have reopened 651 of 773 affected madrasahs (84%), 883 of 1,173 pesantrens (75%), and 1,553 of 1,593 places of worship (97%), a recovery attributed to Religious Affairs Ministry volunteer teams that erected emergency classrooms and provided immediate assistance. The ministry has proposed Rp702.98 billion (about US$41.6 million) under a Special Presidential Directive to rehabilitate and relocate damaged madrasahs, pesantrens, and worship sites in disaster-prone areas; the Nov. 2025 disasters in Aceh, North Sumatra and West Sumatra have, as of Feb. 18, resulted in 1,206 deaths and 138 people still missing according to BNPB.

Key facts: 100% of madrasah students continued learning as of Feb. 11, 2026; 90.5% back in normal classes; 651/773 madrasahs (84%) reopened; 883/1,173 pesantrens (75%) resumed activities; 1,553/1,593 places of worship (97%) operational; Rp702.98 billion (~US$41.6m) proposed for rehabilitation and relocation via Special Presidential Directive; disasters struck Aceh, North Sumatra, West Sumatra in late Nov. 2025; BNPB records 1,206 dead and 138 missing as of Feb. 18, 2026.
importance_reasoning: The report describes a large-scale natural disaster in Sumatra with 1,206 confirmed deaths (and 138 missing), which exceeds the >100-deaths threshold for existential importance. The scale of fatalities, widespread damage to schools and religious sites, and ongoing recovery needs make this event globally notable under the policy criteria.

title: Africa: HIV Funding Still Falls Short of Targets After Pledges
summary:
US pauses and donor reductions in 2025 disrupted delivery of HIV medicines and prevention services across many countries, risking reversal of progress toward epidemic control and weakening health systems. UNAIDS projections warn of as many as 6 million additional HIV infections and 4 million AIDS-related deaths by 2029 if gaps persist. In November 2025 the Global Fund raised US$11.34 billion for HIV/AIDS, TB and malaria for 2027–2029—substantial but short of its US$18 billion target—and the US pledged US$4.6 billion (down from US$6 billion), remaining the largest single donor despite the cut.

The funding shortfall has led to stock-outs of antiretroviral therapy and malaria nets, service reductions and potential job losses, and could slow progress toward UNAIDS targets; over 11 million people had unsuppressed viral loads in 2024 and 40.8 million people were living with HIV at the end of 2024 (about 65% in the WHO African region). Stakeholders and experts urge a multi-pronged African response that increases domestic financing (earmarked taxes, social health insurance), leverages public–private partnerships, integrates HIV services into primary care, uses data-driven targeting, pursues pooled regional procurement to lower drug and diagnostic costs, strengthens community involvement and regional collaboration, and diversifies funding to avoid overreliance on a single donor or the “old model” of international aid.

Key facts and new details: Jan 2025 pause in US foreign assistance contributed to immediate service disruptions; UNAIDS estimate of ~6 million additional infections and ~4 million additional deaths by 2029 if unaddressed; Global Fund replenishment raised US$11.34 billion in Nov 2025 vs an US$18 billion target for 2027–2029; US pledge reduced to US$4.6 billion from US$6 billion; 40.8 million people living with HIV at end-2024 and 44.1 million cumulative deaths to date cited in the article; >11 million people had unsuppressed viral loads in 2024; reported ART and prevention commodity stock-outs and scaled-back programmes; recommendations emphasize domestic resource mobilization, pooled procurement, integration into primary care, public–private partnerships, community-led planning and regional cooperation to mitigate funding gaps.
importance_reasoning: This item describes projected large-scale disease spread and mortality: UNAIDS warns of ~6 million additional HIV infections and ~4 million AIDS-related deaths by 2029 if funding gaps persist, with millions currently unsuppressed and widespread service and drug stock-outs. It therefore involves many deaths and a major infectious-disease resurgence risk that could reverse epidemic control across many countries, meeting the API's criteria for existential importance.

title: Hospital patients collapsing while out of sight on corridors, NHS watchdog says
summary:
Patients are being cared for in corridors, storerooms, gyms and other overflow spaces in NHS hospitals, leaving some out of sight of staff and at risk of deterioration or unnoticed medical emergencies. The Health Services Safety Investigations Body (HSSIB) found these temporary care environments pose risks including falls, infections and lack of oxygen; staff reported patients placed on trolleys or beds who had not been assessed or started treatment, and several incidents were reported where patients collapsed and emergency responses were delayed.

Some trusts have introduced mitigations such as emergency call bells, plug sockets for medical equipment and mini nursing stations to make overflow areas calmer and more organised, while others avoid adding such infrastructure because they do not want corridor care to become normalised. Hospitals try to limit stays in temporary care areas to an hour or less, but bed shortages can extend those stays to several days; NHS England calls corridor care unacceptable, the health secretary has pledged to end the practice by 2029, and professional bodies warn of serious harms and system-wide impacts.

Key facts: HSSIB report highlights risks of using corridors, storerooms and gyms for patient care; staff evidence that some patients in overflow areas were unassessed or untreated and that collapses have occurred out of sight; some hospitals have installed call bells, power sockets and mini nursing stations as mitigations while others resist to avoid normalisation; intended short stays in overflow areas can become days due to lack of beds; the Royal College of Emergency Medicine estimates more than 16,600 deaths in England last year were directly linked to delays finding a bed; NHS and the Department of Health and Social Care state corridor care is unacceptable and cite plans to reduce it, with a 2029 pledge to end the practice.
importance_reasoning: The HSSIB report and Royal College estimate link systemic NHS corridor care and delays to a large number of deaths (the Royal College cites ~16,600 deaths related to delays), which exceeds the API’s numeric threshold for existential importance (more than a hundred deaths). While this is a national healthcare-crisis issue rather than a global existential threat (no new pathogen, nuclear conflict, or global-scale escalation), under the stated criteria the scale of mortality makes it existentially important. It is also of high importance as a major public‑health and governance crisis.

title: 'Entire nation consumed by anger': Iran's Jewish lawmaker breaks with regime, blames
summary:
Dr. Homayoun Sameh, the Jewish representative in Iran’s parliament, issued a rare public rebuke of President Masoud Pezeshkian and condemned the government’s violent response to nationwide unrest, warning of deepening public anger after a crackdown that left thousands of protesters dead. He said he had supported and voted for Pezeshkian but blamed the president for the deteriorating situation and urged the government to honor its pledges to the public.

Sameh stressed that hospitals must remain off-limits amid reports of attacks on medical facilities and alleged executions of wounded protesters, acknowledged ongoing killings without confirming numbers, and warned that mounting economic pressure is fueling popular anger. He cautioned that ignoring outrage could perpetuate the cycle of violence and declined to comment on internet shutdowns, directing questions to the Supreme National Security Council.

Outline of key facts: Rare public rebuke by a Jewish MP of Iran’s leadership; Sameh’s direct criticism and stated prior support for President Pezeshkian; references to a deadly crackdown described as leaving thousands dead; allegations of hospital attacks and executions of wounded protesters; insistence on protecting medical facilities; acknowledgement of continued killings but no confirmed casualty figures; call for the government to act on promises and concern about economic pressure driving protests; refusal to address internet shutdowns, deferring to the Supreme National Security Council.
importance_reasoning: The item describes a deadly nationwide crackdown in Iran with reports of "thousands" of protesters killed and allegations of attacks on hospitals and executions — well above the 100-death threshold. It signals large-scale unrest in a major regional power that could destabilize the region or escalate further, so it meets the criteria for existential importance. This is a report of new events (not an opinion piece).

title: Iran protests continue with 538 people killed, activists say – KTBB News, Weather, Talk
summary:
Mass protests across Iran entered a fourteenth day as activists documented widespread unrest and significant casualties. The U.S.-based Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA) reported 538 deaths (490 protesters and 48 security personnel) and about 10,600 arrests, with video from Tehran’s Punak Square and the northeastern city of Mashhad showing large crowds and HRANA recording protests at 574 locations in 185 cities across all 31 provinces.

Iranian security forces reportedly used tear gas and live ammunition to disperse demonstrations amid sustained internet outages lasting more than 60 hours, while the Iranian government has not released official casualty totals. State-aligned Tasnim reported 109 security personnel killed; senior Iranian leaders framed the unrest as driven by foreign-sponsored “rioters” even as President Masoud Pezeshkian pledged to address economic grievances. International reactions included public support for protesters from Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, warnings and offers of assistance from U.S. President Donald Trump, outreach between Netanyahu and U.S. officials, calls from exiled dissidents for continued street action, and a statement from parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf warning that the U.S. military and Israel could be "legitimate targets" if the U.S. strikes Iran.

Key facts: HRANA toll 538 dead (490 protesters, 48 security forces); ~10,600 arrested; 574 protest locations in 185 cities across all 31 provinces; protests on day 14; internet blackout >60 hours (NetBlocks); videos show large crowds in Tehran’s Punak Square and Mashhad; security forces used tear gas and live ammunition; Tasnim reported 109 security personnel killed; Iranian leaders blame foreign interference but also promise economic responses; Netanyahu and U.S. officials commented and Trump offered U.S. support; exiled Reza Pahlavi urged protesters; parliament speaker Mohammad Bagher Qalibaf warned U.S./Israel could be targeted if strikes occur.
importance_reasoning: The reported toll (HRANA: ~538 dead) exceeds the 100-death threshold and the unrest is nationwide (protests in all provinces, thousands arrested) with security forces using live ammunition and extended internet blackouts. International reactions and warnings (comments from Israeli and U.S. officials, threats from Iranian officials) raise risks of regional escalation. These factors meet the criteria for existential importance (large fatalities and potential for wider escalation).

title: Cambodia hopes French maps will vindicate it in Thai row
summary:
Cambodia has formally asked France for help locating and authenticating colonial-era boundary documents and maps, and for expertise and advisory support, in a bid to strengthen its legal position in a long-running border dispute with Thailand. The contested boundary traces back to French protectorate-era maps (first mapped in 1907 using a watershed principle) and centers on territory around the 11th-century Preah Vihear temple, which the International Court of Justice ruled belongs to Cambodia in 1962 and reaffirmed in 2013.

Clashes between Thai and Cambodian forces have escalated since mid-2025, with major outbreaks in July and December, causing at least 149 deaths (mostly civilians) and displacing hundreds of thousands—about 800,000 Cambodian migrants have returned from Thailand. Cambodia has appealed to international bodies, signaling intent to seek an ICJ ruling and having taken the matter to the UN Security Council, while Thailand prefers bilateral talks and has rejected external mediation. France has so far maintained official neutrality; its possible responses range from narrowly technical archival assistance to more politically fraught involvement, and experts warn that substantive endorsement of one side’s interpretation could harden positions and revive colonial grievances.

Salient facts: Cambodia’s PM Hun Manet wrote to French President Macron requesting access to French archives and advisory help; the 817-kilometer border was first mapped by French officials in 1907 using the watershed principle; the Preah Vihear temple has been the flashpoint with ICJ rulings in 1962 and 2013 favoring Cambodia; recent clashes since July led to at least 149 fatalities and mass displacement (roughly 800,000 Cambodian migrants returning from Thailand); Cambodia is pursuing international legal avenues (ICJ, UN Security Council) while Thailand resists third-party mediation; international courts commonly rely on colonial-era maps and records (examples cited include the Radcliffe line and the Pedra Branca case); analysts say France could provide technical document access/authentication to depoliticize the dispute but that perceived endorsement could exacerbate tensions.
importance_reasoning: The item reports at least 149 fatalities and mass displacement from clashes between Cambodia and Thailand (exceeding the 100-death threshold). It describes interstate conflict with internationalization (appeals to the ICJ and UN Security Council, and possible French involvement) that could harden positions and risk escalation. These factors meet the criteria for existential importance.

title: The Iranian crisis is economic too
summary:
Iran’s economy is portrayed as a long-term decline from rapid, oil-fueled growth before the 1979 revolution to chronic stagnation and crisis today, with the revolution, subsequent shocks, and policy choices weakening growth and living standards. Structural dependence on oil, diversion of resources to security and military priorities, comprehensive U.S. sanctions, and the economic dominance of the IRGC—operating a parallel, state-favored corporate sector—are identified as central causes of persistent underperformance, high inflation, rising poverty and widening inequality.

Recent political and regional tensions have exacerbated economic distress: the piece cites a 0.5% GDP contraction in the first half of the 2025–26 fiscal year after 3.7% growth the prior year; per capita income fell 0.6% during 2011–2020; unemployment around 10%; inflation at 49% in October 2025; annual oil revenue about $23 billion despite Iran’s large hydrocarbon reserves; a collapse of the currency from 70 (rial) per $1 in 1979 to 1.4 million per $1 by early 2026; mass unrest in December 2025 with nearly 4,000 reported killed; and additional pressure from U.S. actions including new tariffs and disruptions to the global oil market.

Outline of salient facts: pre-1979 era saw sustained double-digit growth (growth >10% most years to 1975 and >18% in 1976); 1980 GDP contracted by more than 21%; Iran holds roughly 10% of global oil and gas deposits; reported oil production figure of 3.2 billion barrels per day and annual oil revenue of about $23 billion (claimed to be ~$28 billion below an international benchmark); service sector accounts for over half of GDP; per capita income decline of 0.6% in 2011–2020 and about 40% of Iranians at risk of poverty; unemployment ~10% and inflation 49% (Oct 2025); currency depreciation from 70 to 1.4 million rial per $1 since 1979; expansion of IRGC-controlled conglomerates like Khatam-al Anbiya via noncompetitive government contracts; December 2025 protests with nearly 4,000 reported deaths; and U.S. policy moves including a declared 25% tariff on countries doing business with Iran and indirect effects from U.S. actions in Venezuela.
importance_reasoning: The item reports nearly 4,000 deaths in December 2025 unrest—well above the >100 deaths threshold—and describes large-scale political violence, major economic disruption, and effects on global oil markets and sanctions. Because it documents mass fatalities and instability in a major oil producer with geopolitical ramifications, it meets the API’s criteria for existential importance.

title: COMMENTARY: Iran's Khamenei is now ruling by naked power alone
summary:
Iran's revolutionary regime is in a deep crisis as nationwide protests have stripped away the remaining facades of consent that once steadied Ali Khamenei's rule, leaving the state dependent on open coercion. Security forces have used live rounds and expedited prosecutions against demonstrators, moderates within the regime have lost the ability to mediate, and economic collapse—exacerbated by corruption and the Revolutionary Guard’s control of the economy—has widened unrest across society.

External intervention is fraught and unlikely to produce a decisive or humane outcome: President Trump announced a 25% tariff on any country doing business with Iran, which could harm Iran’s revenues and its main oil customer China but may not force political change; military or cyber action is uncertain and could backfire. Ultimately, while escalating repression could eventually topple the republic, history suggests violent coercion can also prolong authoritarian rule, and meaningful change will most likely have to come from inside Iran.

Key facts: by Jan. 13 at least 734 protesters had been killed based on partial hospital reporting (Iran Human Rights estimates true deaths are in the thousands); Iran executed at least 975 people last year; the prosecutor general labeled protesters “enemies of god,” a charge that can carry the death penalty with expedited trials; President Masoud Pezeshkian loosened enforcement of dress and cultural rules, prompting visible social changes (uncovered women, concerts) but failed to stem economic collapse; bazaar traders and broad social groups joined calls for Khamenei’s removal amid a plunging currency and rising costs; the economy is increasingly controlled by Khamenei’s circle and the IRGC; Trump’s 25% tariff targets countries doing business with Iran and could hurt both Iran and trading partners like China, but sanctions or strikes are unlikely to eliminate the IRGC’s capacity to repress.
importance_reasoning: The report documents at least 734 protester deaths (with estimates possibly in the thousands), exceeding the >100-death threshold for existential importance. Large-scale lethal repression combined with political collapse and the potential for wider regional destabilization meets the API's criteria for events that could threaten broad populations or escalate into more dangerous conflict. This is factual reporting of a major, ongoing crisis (not an opinion piece).

title: Trump says Iran killings stopped, Tehran says 'no plan for hanging'
summary:
US President Donald Trump said he had been told by unspecified "very important sources" that killings of protesters in Iran had stopped and that planned executions would not take place, but he offered no details and said the US had yet to verify the claims. Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi told Fox News there would be "no hanging today or tomorrow" and described an atmosphere of calm, while Iranian officials framed the unrest as "terrorist operation" and held a funeral for over 100 security personnel. Rights groups and monitors report a severe crackdown during a five-day internet blackout, with videos showing bodies in a Tehran morgue and allegations of mass unlawful killings.

International reactions included G7 alarm and warnings of further sanctions, temporary closure of the British embassy in Tehran, US embassy staff warnings in Saudi Arabia, and Lufthansa avoiding Iranian and Iraqi airspace. Iran issued defiant warnings about its ability to retaliate after previously targeting the Al Udeid base; prosecutors have vowed fast-track trials and some detainees face capital charges, while rights groups report at least 3,428 protesters killed and more than 10,000 arrested.

Key facts: Trump claimed assurances (unverified) that killings and executions were halted; Iranian FM denied imminent hangings and asserted control; rights organizations (Iran Human Rights, Amnesty) report ~3,428 killed and 10,000+ detained; Monitor NetBlocks recorded a 144-hour internet blackout; verified videos show bodies at Kahrizak morgue; G7 warned of sanctions; British embassy temporarily closed and Lufthansa rerouted flights; prosecutors promised fast-track trials and possible capital punishment; a reported execution (Erfan Soltani) was said by relatives/rights groups to have been postponed.
importance_reasoning: The report describes mass killings with rights groups alleging ~3,428 deaths and 10,000+ detentions, along with mass arrests, reports of executions or fast-track trials, and a multi-day internet blackout. The item meets the explicit threshold of involving more than a hundred deaths and raises risks of wider repression and regional/geopolitical escalation given international responses (G7 warnings, embassy closures, airspace rerouting). Therefore it qualifies as existentially important under the provided criteria.

title: US Military Shoots Down Iranian Drone Near Aircraft Carrier
summary:
A U.S. F-35C from the USS Abraham Lincoln shot down an Iranian Shahed-139 drone in the Arabian Sea after it approached the carrier about 500 miles from Iran; U.S. Central Command said the strike was in self‑defense and no American service members were harmed. Hours later, Iran’s Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps harassed the U.S.-flagged tanker Stena Imperative in the Strait of Hormuz with fast boats and a drone, prompting the guided‑missile destroyer USS McCaul and U.S. air support to escort the vessel and de‑escalate the incident.

The U.S. has recently reinforced its regional presence, deploying the USS Abraham Lincoln and three destroyers along with roughly 5,000 troops; President Trump referenced a prior U.S. strike (Operation Midnight Hammer, June 2025) and warned Iran while keeping diplomatic channels open. The incidents occur amid nationwide Iranian protests that began December 28, 2025, and a harsh government crackdown—HRANA reports over 6,100 deaths and more than 41,800 arrests, while Iranian authorities give a lower death toll—heightening U.S. concerns about regional stability and prompting CENTCOM and the White House to defend U.S. actions.

Outline of key facts: Shahed-139 drone shot down by an F-35C from USS Abraham Lincoln about 500 miles off Iran; no U.S. casualties reported; IRGC boats and a drone threatened the tanker Stena Imperative in the Strait of Hormuz and were met by USS McCaul and U.S. air escort; recent U.S. naval buildup includes Abraham Lincoln plus three destroyers and ~5,000 troops; President Trump cited Operation Midnight Hammer (June 2025) and warned Iran; HRANA documents ~6,126 deaths and 41,800+ arrests in Iran’s protests, while Iranian authorities report 3,117 deaths; CENTCOM and the White House say U.S. forces acted to protect personnel and vessels.
importance_reasoning: The incident combines a direct U.S.-Iran military engagement (a U.S. warplane shooting down an Iranian drone and subsequent IRGC harassment of commercial shipping) amid a U.S. regional military buildup, creating a real risk of escalation between states. In addition, reporting points to thousands of deaths (HRANA’s ~6,100 figure) in nationwide Iranian protests—well above the >100-deaths threshold. Because it involves state-on-state military action with escalation potential and a context of mass lethal repression, this meets the API’s criteria for existential importance.

title: Ambassador Waltz: Trump ready to act to 'stop the slaughter' in Iran
summary:
The United States told the UN Security Council it stands by Iranian protesters, with US Ambassador Mike Waltz saying President Donald Trump has made clear that "all options are on the table" to stop the slaughter. Waltz rejected Tehran's claim that the protests are a foreign plot, described the regime as weaker and afraid of its people, and framed US remarks as support for those in the streets.

Trump has warned he may intervene amid reports that thousands have been killed in the regime’s crackdown, but later told reporters he had been informed the killing was stopping and that there were no plans for executions. Senator Lindsey Graham rejected reports that the president was backing down, insisting decisive action against the Iranian regime remains possible and adding, "Stay tuned."

Outline: US requested the Security Council meeting and publicly backed protesters; Mike Waltz directly accused the regime of lying about foreign interference; reports cite thousands killed in a crackdown on protests; Trump both threatened intervention and later said killings appeared to be stopping; Lindsey Graham disputed suggestions the administration had softened its stance and signaled continued readiness for action.
importance_reasoning: Reports of thousands killed in a regime crackdown meet the death-threshold criterion (>>100). In addition, US officials signalling that “all options are on the table” and threats of intervention raise the risk of major interstate conflict involving a nuclear-capable country and a leading power, which could escalate regionally or globally. Therefore this item is of existential importance and also clearly highly important.

title: Unintended Confessions: The IRGC Exposed Its Own Crimes During the 2026 Uprising
summary:
Brigadier General Hassan Hassanzadeh, commander of the IRGC’s Greater Tehran forces, is quoted as admitting that “a significant number of innocent people who were merely commuting were martyred,” framing this remark as an implicit acknowledgement of indiscriminate live fire on civilians during Iran’s 2026 nationwide uprising. The piece argues that this statement undermines the regime’s narrative that the unrest was driven by “terrorism,” portraying the killings as evidence of systematic state violence and a deepening crisis of legitimacy.

The article further contends that Hassanzadeh’s reference to “attacks by angry people on IRGC and Basij forces” demonstrates widespread public anger and the erosion of the regime’s aura of invincibility, asserting that the uprising is rooted in long-accumulated domestic grievances rather than foreign interference. It frames the admission as a political and moral turning point—strengthening the Iranian Resistance, exposing organized repression, and signaling a generational shift that could hasten the regime’s downfall.

Outline of salient facts: Brigadier General Hassan Hassanzadeh’s quoted line acknowledging that many “innocent people… were martyred”; characterization of those deaths as resulting from uncontrolled or indiscriminate live fire by the IRGC; reported nationwide figures cited in the piece of more than 3,000 killed across 195 cities; mention of attacks on IRGC and Basij forces as evidence of escalating popular resistance; assertion that the uprising is domestic in origin (not foreign-backed); and the article’s claim that this admission collapses the regime’s terrorism narrative, exposes a legitimacy crisis for Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, and marks a strategic victory for the Iranian Resistance.
importance_reasoning: The item reports a large, nationwide uprising with reported fatalities well above the 100-death threshold (≈3,000 killed across 195 cities). That level of mass lethal violence alone meets the API’s criterion for existential importance. Additionally, it describes widespread unrest that could destabilize a major regional power and has potential for regional spillover or escalation; the content is a factual report of events rather than an opinion piece. For these reasons it is classified as existentially important.

title: US military strike on suspected drug boat kills three in eastern Pacific
summary:
The US military carried out another strike on a vessel accused of trafficking drugs in the Eastern Pacific Ocean; US Southern Command said the boat was transiting known narco-trafficking routes and that the strike killed three people, with a linked video showing a boat bursting into flames. The attack raises the reported toll from strikes on alleged drug boats under the Trump administration to at least 148 deaths in at least 43 attacks since early September in the Caribbean Sea and eastern Pacific.

President Donald Trump has framed the operations as part of an armed conflict with Latin American cartels and a necessary escalation to disrupt drug flows, but the administration has provided little public evidence that those killed were narcoterrorists. Critics question the legality and effectiveness of the campaign—highlighting that much fentanyl reaches the US overland from Mexico—and condemned revelations that survivors of an earlier boat attack were killed in a follow-up strike; Republicans largely defend the actions while Democratic lawmakers and legal experts call them unlawful or potentially war crimes.

Outline: 3 people reported killed in the latest strike; US Southern Command posted the claim and linked video; cumulative count cited as at least 148 dead in at least 43 attacks since early September; administration characterizes the campaign as armed conflict with cartels but has offered limited evidence; critics point to legal and effectiveness concerns given fentanyl’s overland routes from Mexico; additional controversy from reports that survivors of a prior attack were killed in a follow-up strike; partisan divide with Republicans defending and Democrats/legal experts condemning.
importance_reasoning: The reported campaign of US strikes on suspected drug boats has a cumulative reported death toll of at least 148, which exceeds the API’s >100-deaths threshold for existential importance. The strikes involve direct military action with significant loss of life and legal/political controversy—factors the criteria treat as globally consequential. While this specific episode is unlikely to threaten humanity as a whole (no new pathogen, nuclear escalation, or comparable global-risk vector), it meets the explicit numeric threshold and thus is categorized as existentially important under the provided rules.

title: Activists confirm 3,919 deaths in Iran protests
importance_reasoning: The report cites 3,919 deaths, which exceeds the 100-death threshold in the policy. An event with thousands of deaths is treated as existentially important due to its massive human toll and potential for major domestic and regional destabilization.

title: The Philippines, ASEAN's new chair, starts 2026 on a 'weaker footing' after trade tensions and a $2 billion corruption scandal
summary:
The Philippines enters 2026 on a weaker economic footing as President Ferdinand “Bongbong” Marcos Jr. assumes the ASEAN chair amid a major corruption scandal and challenging trade dynamics. Investigations revealed about $2 billion in missing flood-management funds, fueling public outrage after Typhoon Kalmaegi caused over 200 deaths and more than $60 million in crop and farmland losses; third-quarter GDP fell to a four-year low of 4%, and Manila has cut growth targets for 2026–2028.

Despite domestic setbacks, the Philippines aims to use its ASEAN leadership to pursue regional priorities—notably signing the ASEAN Digital Economy Framework Agreement (DEFA) to deepen the digital market and strengthen supply chains. The country faces competitive pressure from ASEAN peers and structural limits due to shallow manufacturing depth and heavy reliance on imported intermediate goods; policymakers are advised to rebuild credibility, speed up investment and industrial upgrading, and invest in digital literacy and AI standards to protect the BPO sector and translate 2026’s opportunity into durable gains.

Key facts: ~$2 billion in flood-management funds reportedly disappeared amid probes into misallocated funds, ghost projects and substandard materials; Typhoon Kalmaegi caused 200+ deaths and >$60M in agricultural losses; Q3 GDP slowed to 4% (a four-year low) prompting lowered growth targets; a July U.S.-Philippines trade deal set a 19% tariff on U.S.-bound exports while removing some tariffs on U.S. goods (Vietnam and Malaysia now have comparable tariffs at ~20% and 19%); the Philippines’ limited manufacturing depth—heavy dependence on imported intermediate goods—risks missing supply-chain reconfiguration; DEFA could create a ~$2 trillion unified Southeast Asian digital market and boost the BPO sector; AI threatens BPO jobs without retraining; success hinges on credibility, execution, governance and the speed of adjustment.
importance_reasoning: The report notes Typhoon Kalmaegi caused over 200 deaths. Per the API criteria (items involving more than 100 deaths count as existentially important), this meets the threshold. Other elements (a $2B corruption scandal, ASEAN chairmanship, trade tensions, DEFA and digital-economy issues) are regionally significant and highly important, but the death toll is the decisive factor for existential classification.

title: How Iran protest death tolls have been politicised
summary:
Ayatollah Ali Khamenei accused the US and Israel of fomenting recent anti-government protests in Iran, which were met with a brutal government crackdown amid a communications blackout that has hindered independent verification. Reporting describes a shift from initial attempts by authorities to placate protesters to renewed claims that demonstrations were hijacked by violent, externally directed actors, alongside efforts to intimidate and suppress dissent.

Estimates of fatalities vary widely—from several hundred to more than 20,000—reflecting differences in methodology and the constraints of the communications blackout. The Human Rights Activists News Agency (HRANA) reports over 4,000 confirmed deaths with 9,000 under review; a separate doctors’ report inside Iran suggests up to 20,000. An Iranian official later said authorities had verified at least 5,000 dead, including about 500 security personnel, after an earlier government figure of 2,000. Circulating videos of body bags outside Tehran morgues and commentary accusing the regime of psychological warfare have intensified debate, while some commentators accuse Western media of inconsistent scrutiny compared with coverage of other conflicts.

Key facts: Khamenei blamed the US and Israel for the unrest; a communications blackout has limited independent verification; HRANA: >4,000 confirmed fatalities + 9,000 under review; an internal doctors’ report suggests up to 20,000 killed; government figures shifted from ~2,000 to a claimed verified 5,000 (including ~500 security personnel); videos show long lines of body bags at morgues; allegations of state-sponsored intimidation and “psychological warfare”; debate over methodological differences in death estimates and accusations of Western media double standards.
importance_reasoning: Reports describe thousands of deaths (estimates ranging from several thousand to up to ~20,000) in a large-scale government crackdown on nationwide protests. The reported fatality counts far exceed the 100-death threshold; the crisis also involves a communications blackout and major human-rights abuses with potential to destabilize the country and region. This meets the criteria for existential importance (large loss of life and risk of broad destabilization).

title: Balochistan announces Rs1.38b bounty for 39 'most-wanted terrorists'
summary:
Balochistan authorities published names and photographs of 39 individuals in major newspapers, labeling them "most-wanted terrorists" and offering cash rewards for information leading to their arrest. Among those named are Bashir Zeb, identified as head of the proscribed Baloch Liberation Army (BLA), and Gulzar Imam (aka Shambay), a key commander of the Majeed Brigade; each carries a bounty of Rs250 million. The provincial government said credible, actionable tips would be acted on promptly, assured confidentiality for informants, and provided two helpline numbers (1719 and 1218) to facilitate public cooperation as part of intensified efforts to dismantle militant networks in Balochistan.

The announcement comes amid a long-running insurgency in the province and follows a recent attack attributed to the banned group ‘Fitna-al-Hindustan’ that killed dozens; a subsequent security operation reportedly killed 216 militants while 22 security personnel were martyred.

Key facts: 39 people named as "most-wanted"; top bounties of Rs250 million each for Bashir Zeb (BLA leader) and Gulzar Imam/Shambay (Majeed Brigade commander); total reward pool Rs1.38 billion; half-page "Most Wanted Terrorists" advertisement ran in leading newspapers; helplines 1719 and 1218 provided; informant identities to be kept confidential; both top targets reported from Kech district; context includes recent Fitna-al-Hindustan attack and a counter-operation with 216 militants killed and 22 security personnel killed.
importance_reasoning: The item reports a counter‑operation that reportedly killed 216 militants and 22 security personnel (roughly 238 deaths) and describes 39 named ‘most‑wanted’ terrorists and large bounties. It therefore meets the >100‑deaths threshold and involves militant/terrorist actors that could materially destabilize the region, so it is judged existentially important under the stated criteria. (It does not, however, indicate nuclear escalation or a global pandemic threat.)

title: Does Trump Have the Legal Authority to Strike Iran? An Expert Weighs In
summary:
President Donald Trump has built up a substantial U.S. military presence in the Middle East and said he is considering limited strikes against Iran to pressure its leadership into accepting a nuclear deal. Two aircraft carriers are deployed and the USS Gerald Ford is expected to arrive imminently; Trump gave Tehran a 10–15 day deadline and has previously pointed to strikes in June 2025 that hit Iranian nuclear sites. The prospect of further action follows a violent crackdown on protests in Iran and has drawn criticism from lawmakers inside and outside his party.

Reps. Thomas Massie and Ro Khanna plan to introduce a War Powers resolution to bar the president from ordering strikes on Iran without congressional approval. David Janovsky, Acting Director of The Constitution Project, tells TIME that contemplated strikes would lack lawful justification absent an imminent attack, that the administration would need to seek congressional authorization, and that the June 2025 strikes were likewise legally questionable. Janovsky argues a formal congressional prohibition would change legal counsel calculations, constrain military lawyers, and alter the political dynamics that have produced muted congressional pushback.

Key facts and developments: Trump said, "I guess I can say I am considering that," and set a 10–15 day deadline for Iran; two carriers are already deployed and the USS Gerald Ford is expected soon; the U.S. participated in an Israeli-led operation in June 2025 that struck three Iranian nuclear sites; Reps. Massie and Khanna will push a War Powers resolution (Massie posted about forcing a House vote on X on Feb. 18); Janovsky’s legal assessment: unilateral strikes now would be illegal absent an imminent attack and Congress would need to authorize any act of war; a preemptive congressional prohibition could blunt executive branch legal arguments and affect military and political calculations; muted congressional reaction is attributed to long-term structural acquiescence and a past assumption that the 2025 strikes were a one-off.
importance_reasoning: This reports an active U.S. military posture and consideration of strikes on Iran (deployment of two carriers, prior strikes on Iranian nuclear sites, a president setting deadlines), which is a concrete potential interstate military escalation involving a nuclear-armed state. Such actions could escalate regionally or internationally and therefore meet the threshold for existential importance. This is reporting new events and policy decisions (not an opinion piece).

title: President Mahama Vindicates Martin Amidu By Admitting That Asantehene's Bawku Conflict Mediation Failed
summary:
Martin A. B. K. Amidu condemns President John Mahama’s handling of the Bawku chieftaincy dispute, arguing the President abdicated his constitutional duty by relying on the Asantehene’s mediation. Amidu notes Mahama’s admission on 10 February 2026 that the mediation “failed to produce” a traditional settlement, stresses that mediation cannot substitute for arbitration or judicial adjudication, and asserts the government’s acceptance and purported implementation of the Asantehene’s report lack constitutional and legal authority.

Amidu contends that the government’s failure to enforce existing law between 7 January 2025 and 24 December 2025 contributed to avoidable violence and deaths, calls for an apology and accountability, and warns the Asantehene-led mediation escalated the conflict by involving the Nayiri and sidelining proper legal processes. He reiterates prior publications demanding clarity on whether arbitration or mediation was intended and argues the mediation has politicized rather than pacified Bawku.

Outline of salient facts: President Mahama admitted the Asantehene’s mediation failed at a 10 February 2026 meeting with the Upper East Regional House of Chiefs; the Asantehene formally presented the Bawku Mediation Report on 16 December 2025 and the government issued an official statement accepting its findings despite Amidu’s earlier calls (mid-December 2025) for clarity on whether a binding arbitration or a mediator’s report was intended; Mahama estimated about 119 people died during the mediation period; Amidu alleges Alhaji Seidu Abagre was taken to Accra without a court warrant to enable the mediation; Amidu argues the mediator usurped adjudicative functions (making the report constitutionally and legally infirm), that the government’s inaction transformed and escalated the conflict by bringing the Nayiri into it, and that there should be accountability and an apology for the lives lost.
importance_reasoning: The report describes a localized chieftaincy conflict in which the government estimates about 119 people died during the mediation period. The casualty count exceeds the >100-deaths threshold defined for existential importance. Additionally, the admitted failure of mediation, allegations of unlawful actions and political manipulation increase the risk of further escalation and rule-of-law breakdown. For these reasons it meets the API's criteria for existential importance.

title: US and Iran gather for high stakes nuclear talks
summary:
Senior U.S. and Iranian officials met in Oman to discuss Tehran’s disputed nuclear program amid a renewed U.S. military buildup and threats of strike. The talks—the first since a 12‑day war with Israel and subsequent U.S./Israeli strikes on Iranian nuclear sites—brought U.S. special envoy Steve Witkoff, Jared Kushner and Adm. Brad Cooper together with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi; President Trump signaled interest in a deal but warned he has other options.

Washington’s aims include ending Iran’s uranium enrichment, curbing its ballistic‑missile program and stopping support for regional proxies, while Iran has said it will only negotiate its nuclear work and shows little readiness to halt enrichment. Iran is portrayed as weakened after strike damage to enrichment equipment, widespread domestic protests and economic distress, yet its leaders have dug in and recent hostile incidents (a drone launched at a U.S. carrier and harassment of a U.S.‑flagged tanker) underscore the fragility of the situation; disputes over venue and agenda (initially Turkey, settled in Oman) and low expectations from analysts leave a follow‑on meeting — not a comprehensive deal — as the most likely positive outcome.

Outline of salient facts: meeting in Oman after June’s 12‑day war and U.S./Israeli strikes on nuclear sites; U.S. delegation included Witkoff, Jared Kushner and Adm. Brad Cooper; Iran represented by Abbas Araghchi; U.S. demands: stop enrichment, limit missiles, end proxy support; Iran’s stance: negotiate only nuclear issues and resist ending enrichment; recent escalations: drone launched at a U.S. carrier and gunboat harassment of a tanker; Iran’s enrichment equipment damaged or buried, domestic protests brutally suppressed, economy and currency weakened; venue/format disputes (Turkey vs Oman) exposed substantive gaps; analysts say expectations are low, with the best realistic outcome a scheduled follow‑up meeting.
importance_reasoning: High‑level U.S.–Iran negotiations over Tehran’s nuclear program, coming after strikes on Iranian nuclear sites, a U.S. military buildup, and recent hostile incidents (a drone attack on a U.S. carrier, harassment of a tanker), present a risk of escalation between a major power and a regional adversary over nuclear weapons. Such tensions involving nuclear programs and potential military escalation could spill beyond the region or provoke wider military responses, meeting the criterion of an event that could threaten large‑scale international security. Therefore this is existentially important.

title: Iran's top diplomat hopeful for negotiations even amid US military buildup
summary:
Iran and the United States are engaged in indirect talks over Tehran’s nuclear program amid heightened military tension, with Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi saying he is ‘‘confident that we can achieve a deal’’ while refusing to promise direct talks. President Trump said Iran was ‘‘seriously talking’’ and noted US ships and forces were positioned nearby; Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei warned that any US strike would trigger a regional war.

Negotiations are stalled by Iran’s demand that talks concentrate solely on nuclear issues and the US refusal to reduce its regional military presence. Tehran expects lifting of US sanctions and continued peaceful enrichment if a deal is reached; Araghchi warned Iran would be ready for war if talks fail and said US bases would be targets. The situation is further complicated by Iran’s rebuilt missile arsenal and regional proxies (including the Houthis), widespread domestic unrest after January protests (human rights groups estimate several thousand killed), government denials of executions and claims the unrest was driven by ‘‘terrorist elements,’’ and recent diplomatic moves such as a visit by Qatar’s prime minister to Tehran and a call between Iran’s president Masoud Pezeshkian and Egypt’s Abdel Fattah El-Sisi.

Key facts: Araghchi’s public confidence in a deal and insistence on focusing talks on nuclear issues; Khamenei’s warning that a US strike would lead to regional war; Trump’s comments that Iran is ‘‘talking’’ and mention of US ships near Iran; Iran’s demand for sanctions relief and the right to peaceful enrichment; US insistence on maintaining military presence in the region; reports that Iran’s missile forces were largely rebuilt after last year’s 12-day war with Israel and that proxies like the Houthis threaten regional shipping; Araghchi’s warning that US bases could be targeted if conflict resumes; human rights groups’ estimate of several thousand killed in recent protests and Iranian denials of planned executions; recent diplomatic outreach by Qatar and Egypt aimed at de-escalation.
importance_reasoning: The report describes several thousand protest deaths (exceeds the >100-death threshold) and describes heightened US–Iran military tensions around Iran’s nuclear program, US forces nearby, rebuilt missile forces, proxy threats (e.g., Houthis), and explicit warnings that a US strike could trigger regional war. These factors create a credible risk of major regional escalation and potential broader conflict involving a nuclear-armed power and a state with a contentious nuclear program, meeting the criteria for existential importance.

title: Iranian press review: Calls grow for sanctions relief across political divide
summary:
Moderate Iranian figures and international-affairs experts are urging rapid progress in a new round of negotiations with the US to lift sanctions, with Gholamhossein Karbaschi making a direct appeal to President Masoud Pezeshkian and Ali Bigdeli arguing that talks must reflect current realities; Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has not publicly commented, a silence interpreted by some as tacit approval.

Figures close to the political establishment are also calling for recognition of a widespread “collective grief” after the violent suppression of nationwide protests, highlighting a deep social crisis: official fatalities are listed at 3,117 while independent groups say the toll may exceed 6,000, and advisers warn that unresolved grief, economic hardship and fears of conflict could spark renewed unrest.

Outline of salient facts: Direct public appeals for ending sanctions from Karbaschi and strategic criticism from Bigdeli; Khamenei’s silence on the talks. Public calls by Saeed Khademi and Pirouz Hanachi to acknowledge national mourning and prevent further social tension. A rapid wave of arrests and summonses targeting reformist figures — reported detainees include Javad Imam, Ghorban Behzadiannejad, Hossein Karroubi, Ali Shakouri‑Rad, Azar Mansouri and Ebrahim Asgharzadeh, with others (Mohsen Armin, Badr al‑Sadat Mofidi, Feyzollah Arab Sorkh) summoned — described as a scale and speed not seen since the 2009 Green Movement. Lawyers, led by Hamidreza Haji Esfandiari and a group issuing an open letter, are protesting extralegal restrictions and citing violations of Article 35: blocked access to approved counsel, rushed minutes‑long trials, broadcast forced confessions and extraction of confessions under pressure.
importance_reasoning: The report documents a very large death toll (officially 3,117; independent counts ~6,000), far exceeding the >100-deaths threshold. The scale of killings, mass arrests, and nationwide unrest creates a significant risk of prolonged instability and large-scale human suffering; under the API rules, events with this magnitude of fatalities are classified as existentially important. While this is not a nuclear or pandemic event, the casualty numbers and potential for broad societal collapse/continued lethal repression meet the existential-importance criteria.

title: Sudan civil war: Ethiopia's, UAE's role under scrutiny
summary:
A Reuters investigation reports that Ethiopia is hosting a covert camp in the Benishangul-Gumuz border region to house and train thousands of fighters for Sudan’s Rapid Support Forces (RSF), with satellite imagery showing tented facilities. The report links the camp’s construction and training support to the United Arab Emirates (UAE), though Reuters could not independently verify UAE involvement and the UAE denied participation.

Regional analysts quoted in the piece say the RSF now has backing from the UAE and elements in Libya, Chad and Ethiopia, while the Sudanese Armed Forces are supported by Egypt, Saudi Arabia and Somalia, entrenching a stalemate that makes a decisive end to the conflict unlikely. Ethiopia has also taken in more than 50,000 Sudanese refugees in 2024 while coping with its own internal displacement of some 3.5 million people.

Key facts and figures: Reuters-published satellite imagery identifies tented training/housing sites in western Ethiopia; allegations (unverified by Reuters) that the UAE financed and provided trainers/logistics for the camp, with the UAE denying involvement; analysts characterise the UAE as a principal external backer of the RSF. Humanitarian toll and context: conflict began April 2023 after power struggles following Sudan’s 2019 transition and the 2021 military coup; RSF roots in Janjaweed militias. UN estimates over 40,000 killed and about 14 million displaced; WHO describes the situation as the world’s worst health and humanitarian crisis—20+ million need health assistance, 21+ million need food, and an estimated 33.7 million will need humanitarian aid this year. IPC reports famine levels in parts of Darfur; WHO documents 201 attacks on health care since April 2023 with 1,858 deaths and 490 injuries.
importance_reasoning: The report describes a large-scale, ongoing conflict with UN estimates of over 40,000 killed and millions displaced, meeting the casualty/scale threshold for existential importance. It also documents major humanitarian collapse (tens of millions needing aid), external state involvement and proxy support that raises the risk of regional escalation. These factors could threaten large populations and destabilize the region, so the item is of existential importance.

title: Satellite pics reveal HUGE US military buildup near Iran as Trump 'lays groundwork' for attack after warning Ayatollah
importance_reasoning: Describes a large US military buildup near Iran combined with rhetoric about a possible strike. An imminent US-Iran military confrontation could escalate regionally, draw in other states, disrupt global energy and shipping, and risk wider conflict involving major powers — outcomes that threaten global stability. Therefore it meets the threshold for existential importance.

title: Migrant arrivals in the United Kingdom rose in 2025 while numbers in the EU stabilised
summary:
Migrant arrivals to the United Kingdom via the English Channel reached 41,472 in 2025, about 5,000 more than in 2024, making 2025 the second-highest year on record after 2022’s peak of 45,774. Across the European Union, Frontex reported a 25% decline in irregular entries in the first eleven months of 2025, with contrasting regional trends.

Outline: UK Channel crossings 41,472 in 2025 (second highest, behind 2022’s 45,774); Frontex recorded over 166,900 irregular entries into the EU Jan–Nov 2025 (down 25%); sharp declines on West African routes (-60%), Western Balkans (-43%) and Eastern Mediterranean (-43%); western Mediterranean route (Algeria/Morocco–Spain) up 15%; Central Mediterranean busiest corridor with about 63,200 arrivals (nearly 40% of irregular entries), departures mainly from Libya; IOM reports more than 1,700 deaths attempting Mediterranean crossings in 2025.
importance_reasoning: The report cites more than 1,700 deaths attempting Mediterranean crossings in 2025, which exceeds the threshold of 100 deaths for existential importance. The large scale of fatalities and substantial irregular migration flows present major humanitarian and regional-security implications.

title: Islamic State group claims deadly attack on Niamey airport in Niger
summary:
Islamic State’s regional affiliate claimed responsibility for a coordinated surprise attack on Niamey airport in Niger, saying it inflicted heavy losses. The attackers reportedly arrived on motorcycles; Nigerien security forces repelled the assault, killing 20 attackers and arresting 11. Material damage at the airport included a stash of ammunition that caught fire and several civilian aircraft that were damaged, and state television showed footage suggesting one of the dead assailants was a French national (unverified).

The IS-linked group has been tied to recent high-profile violence in Niger, including strikes in the Tillaberi region that killed more than 120 people and the October abduction of an American pilot. The attack underscores persistent instability in the Sahel, where Niger’s 2023 military coup leaders have cut ties with France, accused neighbouring governments of backing militants, and turned to Russia for military support.

Outline of key facts: IS affiliate claimed the Niamey airport attack via Amaq; attackers arrived on motorcycles; security forces killed 20 attackers and detained 11; an ammunition cache burned and several civilian planes were damaged; one attacker reported as French by state TV (no independent evidence); IS-linked militants previously carried out deadly strikes in Tillaberi (120+ killed) and abducted a U.S. pilot; the incident occurs amid Niger’s junta distancing from France and seeking Russian support since the 2023 coup.
importance_reasoning: The report describes an Islamic State regional affiliate carrying out a coordinated attack on Niamey airport with significant material damage and casualties among the attackers, and it explicitly references recent IS-linked strikes in the region that killed more than 120 people. This both documents a campaign that has already produced mass casualties (meeting the >100 deaths criterion) and shows a militant group striking critical infrastructure and sustaining high-casualty operations in the Sahel. Those factors meet the API's thresholds for existential importance because they indicate large-scale lethal violence and a terrorist group displaying capabilities that threaten regional stability and could escalate further.

title: Iran Insistent On Uranium Enrichment As US Navy Flotilla Sails In Arabian Sea
summary:
Iran’s foreign minister, Abbas Araqchi, insisted that Iran will not accept “zero enrichment” and defended its right to enrich uranium as a matter of independence and dignity after talks with U.S. officials in Oman over Tehran’s nuclear programs. The Oman discussions took place amid a U.S. military buildup in the region — including the deployment of the USS Abraham Lincoln carrier strike group — and during widespread, violently suppressed domestic protests in Iran.

Following the talks, the U.S. announced new sanctions targeting Iran’s oil exports and related shadow-fleet vessels. Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu planned to meet President Trump to discuss negotiations with Iran; White House envoy Steve Witkoff and the head of U.S. Central Command visited the carrier, and Jared Kushner has been closely involved in the diplomatic efforts.

Outline of salient facts: Abbas Araqchi declared “zero enrichment” unacceptable and framed enrichment as tied to national independence; Oman talks kept both sides engaged and were called a “step forward” by Iran’s President Masoud Pezeshkian; U.S. positioned the USS Abraham Lincoln and accompanying ships in the region; the State Department imposed sanctions on 15 entities and 14 shadow-fleet vessels tied to Iran’s oil exports; Iran is experiencing its largest protests since 1979 with rights groups reporting thousands killed; Netanyahu scheduled a Feb. 11 meeting with Trump to discuss Iran; U.S. envoy Witkoff and CENTCOM leadership visited the carrier, and Jared Kushner remains a central figure in negotiations.
importance_reasoning: The report cites rights groups reporting thousands killed in widespread protests (exceeding the >100-death threshold) and describes Iran’s insistence on uranium enrichment alongside U.S. naval deployment, sanctions, and high-level diplomatic/military engagements. Those elements (large-scale fatalities plus a contentious nuclear program and major-power military positioning with Israeli and U.S. involvement) create a credible risk of regional escalation with global implications, so it meets the API’s existential-importance criteria.

title: Netanyahu races to Washington for emergency Trump meeting amid Israel's fears over high stakes US-Iran talks
summary:
Benjamin Netanyahu has asked to bring forward a visit to Washington originally scheduled for 18 February, rushing to meet President Donald Trump amid Israeli concern about recent US talks with Iran. Israel fears a narrow deal focused only on nuclear enrichment could leave Tehran’s ballistic missile programme and support for groups such as Hezbollah and Hamas untouched, and is pressing that any agreement address those wider security threats.

An initial round of US–Iran talks took place in Oman with Jared Kushner and Steve Witkoff representing the US; the discussions follow years of stalled negotiations since the 2015 deal was abandoned. The US has also increased its naval presence near Iran, which Trump described as an “armada,” while Iran faces a violent domestic crackdown with a death toll the article estimates in the tens of thousands. Vice-President J.D. Vance said diplomacy with Iran is complicated by its political structure and suggested Trump prefers non-military options but could choose military action if he sees no alternative.

Outline of salient facts: Netanyahu moved his Washington visit forward amid concern the US will conclude an unfavorable Iran deal; Oman talks involved Kushner and special envoy Steve Witkoff; Israel demands any deal include limits on ballistic missiles and an end to Iranian support for militant groups as well as nuclear constraints; US officials (including Marco Rubio, named in the article) urge talks go beyond nuclear issues; Trump described the US fleet as an “armada” and has repeatedly threatened military action; the article notes little progress since Trump abandoned the 2015 agreement and highlights Iran’s severe domestic crackdown and comments from VP J.D. Vance about the limits of diplomacy.
importance_reasoning: The item reports high-stakes US–Iran diplomacy amid increased military posturing (US naval buildup, threats of military action) and cites a violent Iranian domestic crackdown with an estimated death toll in the tens of thousands. It therefore meets the >100 deaths threshold and describes a situation with a significant risk of international military escalation between major powers and their proxies, so it is of existential importance.

title: Mullah's revenge on 'enemies of God': Hundreds of protesters killed in Iran as hardline regime fights for survival
summary:
Widespread anti-government demonstrations that began on December 28 over soaring inflation have spread across all 31 of Iran’s provinces and escalated into nationwide demands to end clerical rule. Security forces have carried out a harsh crackdown; hospital staff, activists and a Tehran doctor report hundreds of protesters killed and many more injured, with medics describing live ammunition wounds and direct shots to heads and chests, and several hospitals overwhelmed.

Authorities imposed an almost complete internet and telephone blackout after about 8pm and switched off public lighting as videos and images circulated showing fires, overturned cars and attacks on government buildings in Tehran, Karaj, Rasht, Tabriz, Shiraz and Kerman. Political and security figures have threatened harsh penalties — leaders declared participants “enemies of God” and the prosecutor-general suggested protesters could face the death penalty — while the Revolutionary Guards’ intelligence unit said it arrested a foreigner suspected of spying.

Outline of key facts: protests across all 31 provinces; protests began Dec. 28 over inflation and moved to anti-clerical demands; medics and three hospitals report being overwhelmed and at least 217 deaths recorded by six Tehran hospitals (per a doctor), with many killed by live rounds; internet/phone blackout affecting ~85 million people and public lighting turned off; reports of fires and attacks on 25 mosques, 26 banks and the Basij headquarters (per Tehran’s mayor); senior officials and the Revolutionary Guards signaling severe reprisals; reports that the White House has held preliminary discussions and US options (including a possible large-scale strike) were cited; rumours of senior clerics fleeing or the Supreme Leader considering departure.
importance_reasoning: Reports describe hundreds of protesters killed (medical staff and hospitals report at least ~217 deaths), a nationwide uprising across all provinces, a near-complete communications blackout, and signals of severe reprisals by security forces. The confirmed death toll alone passes the >100-deaths threshold for existential importance; the scale, nationwide instability, disruption of communications, and potential for regional or international escalation (including mentions of foreign involvement/options) further justify categorizing this as existentially important.

title: 'Rule of lies': Foreign Ministry slams 'Hitler-fashioned disinformation campaign' against Iran
summary:
Iranian Foreign Ministry spokesman Esmail Baghaei accused Israeli media of fabricating casualty reports from recent violent unrest, sharing a viral video of a Hebrew-speaking woman, Noya (Noa) Zion, who appears on camera showing her own photo and stating she is alive and never traveled to Iran after Israel’s Channel 12 reportedly identified her as a victim. Baghaei characterized the incident as part of a broader disinformation campaign, invoking the German term große Lüge to describe what he called a systematic campaign of false narratives against Iran.

Iranian officials have repeatedly criticized Western outlets for inflating casualty figures from the unrest — some Western reports claim up to 80,000 deaths — while Iranian official records report 3,117 fatalities. Of that total, the government identifies 2,427 as martyrs, innocent civilians and security personnel killed by terrorists. Iranian authorities blame the violence on foreign-backed groups including the Mojahedin Khalq Organization (MKO), monarchists and armed separatists, and say those groups launched armed attacks on January 8–9 intended to create mass casualties and destabilize urban centers.

Outline of salient facts: Baghaei shared a video of Noya (Noa) Zion denying she was killed; Israel’s Channel 12 is reported to have broadcast her photo as a victim; Baghaei described the tactic as a große Lüge (Big Lie) and part of systematic disinformation; some Western reports allege up to 80,000 deaths while Iran’s official toll is 3,117; the government lists 2,427 as martyrs, civilians and security personnel killed by terrorists; Iran blames the MKO, monarchists and separatist groups for armed attacks on Jan 8–9 aimed at mass casualties.
importance_reasoning: The item describes violent unrest with reported fatalities well above the API’s threshold (official toll 3,117; some reports claim up to 80,000), and alleges organized/foreign-backed attacks and systematic disinformation. Those casualty figures alone meet the “more than a hundred deaths” criterion for existential importance, and the involvement of interstate tensions (Iran/Israel) and potential for escalation further raise the severity. It does not describe a pandemic or nuclear exchange, but under the stated rules the scale and geopolitical risk qualify it as existentially important.

title: US vice president to chair national security meeting on Iran amid demonstrations
summary:
U.S. Vice President JD Vance will chair a meeting of National Security Council principals at 4pm Eastern to discuss the rapidly evolving situation in Iran amid continued mass demonstrations. It is not yet clear whether President Donald Trump will participate in the meeting in person or by phone as he returns to Washington from Detroit.

Trump publicly urged Iranian anti-government demonstrators to continue protesting, saying "help is on its way," and announced he had canceled meetings with Iranian officials until the killings of protesters stop; he declined to specify what aid might be provided. The unrest follows a deteriorating Iranian economy and a rapidly depreciating rial, and the Human Rights Activists News Agency estimates at least 646 dead (including security forces and protesters) and over 1,000 injured.

Outline of salient facts: Vance to chair NSC principals meeting at 4pm ET; Trump’s participation unclear and could be via Air Force One; Trump urged protesters to "keep protesting," used the acronym "MIGA," and canceled meetings with Iranian officials; Trump was noncommittal about the nature of any aid; protests linked to economic collapse and rial depreciation; HRANA estimates at least 646 fatalities and 1,000+ injuries.
importance_reasoning: The report cites an HRANA estimate of at least 646 deaths in mass protests in Iran, which exceeds the >100-deaths threshold for existential importance. The situation has prompted a U.S. National Security Council principals meeting and possible U.S. involvement, increasing the potential for broader regional or international implications. Therefore this item meets the criteria for existential importance.

title: Iran ready to consider compromises to reach nuclear deal, minister tells BBC in Tehran
summary:
Iran says it is willing to consider compromises to reach a nuclear deal with the US if Washington is prepared to discuss lifting sanctions, Majid Takht-Ravanchi, Iran’s deputy foreign minister, told the BBC.
